rows.value: Delegation of Authority (DOA) – Policy and Matrix

body.text: This Delegation of Authority (DOA) matrix establishes a formal governance framework defining who has the authority to approve, commit, and sign off on decisions at Bridger Aerospace — and up to what dollar amount or scope. Authority is assigned by role and title, not by individual, so the framework survives personnel changes.

This document is both a governance instrument and a direct response to documented past failures, including unauthorized service agreements, unchecked vendor commitments, and contracts executed without cross-departmental review. It is intended to give employees clear guidance on when they can act independently and when they must escalate.
This policy applies to all employees, officers, and contractors acting on behalf of Bridger Aerospace across the following decision categories:

items: Financial – payments, journal entries, budget variances, capital expenditures
Contracts & Legal – vendor contracts, government/agency contracts, NDAs, and commercial agreements.
Operations & Safety – maintenance approvals, return-to-service, AOG decisions, Q/A sign-offs
Human Resources & Administrative – hiring, termination, compensation, IT system access
Business Development – bids, LOIs, MOUs, LOUs, joint ventures, and acquisitions

body.text: This framework operates within and does not supersede applicable regulatory requirements, including FAR Part 119 (FAA), federal contract requirements (USFS, BLM), and SOX internal controls.

Bridger Aerospace operates under FAA certification as an aerial firefighting operator. Certain authority structures in the Operations & Safety section are not discretionary; they reflect the regulatory minimum under FAR Part 119 and the company’s Operations Specification (OpSpec A007). Key principles:

items: Return-to-service authority flows through the repair station roster, authorized by the Chief Inspector.
Authority may be delegated, but FAA responsibility cannot be transferred.
When delegation occurs (e.g., unavailability of Chief Inspector), communication is required — email, out-of-office notification, or equivalent written record.
Escalation chain for Q/A decisions: Chief Inspector → DOM (Director of Maintenance) → AE (Airworthiness Engineer).

body.text: Bridger Aerospace is subject to SOX, confirmed by IT/SOX Compliance (Sam Bennett). Sections 302 and 404 require effective internal controls over financial reporting, of which this DOA matrix is a foundational component. Specific implications:

items: Segregation of duties is required for all financially sensitive actions. The individual who initiates a transaction may not also approve it.

Financial system access controls are governed by the SaaS Ownership Appointment Memorandum (signed by CEO, June 24, 2025). Business owners approve new access; IT creates it. An owner may not create their own user access.
Dual approval is required for journal entries above the materiality threshold (threshold pending confirmation from Finance) and for all top-side adjusting entries.
Audit trails must be maintained for all approvals. Overrides of this matrix must be documented with reason, approver, and date.

body.text: USFS and BLM tanker contracts carry significant legal, operational, and financial obligations. Government and agency contracts require CEO signature and Legal review. Novel contract types outside Bridger’s normal service portfolio require CEO approval before pursuit.
All authority in this matrix is assigned to roles and titles, not to named individuals. When an incumbent changes, authority passes to the new holder of the role.

If the authorized role-holder is unavailable (out of office, conflict of interest, or otherwise unable to act), the decision escalates to the next level in the escalation path shown in the matrix. For FAA-regulated roles, unavailability must be communicated in writing.
Until a formal unbudgeted approval process is established, all unbudgeted expenditures require CEO approval (Sam Davis, per his direct instruction). This applies regardless of dollar amount.

Any commitment, agreement, or expenditure made without the authority described in this matrix is unauthorized and may expose the company to legal, financial, and regulatory liability. Past instances — including unauthorized service agreements, brokerage arrangements, and software vendor signups — are the documented basis for this policy.

Where dollar thresholds in this draft are marked [PENDING], the conservative interpretation applies: treat the item as requiring the next higher level of approval until thresholds are confirmed. This document will be revised when outstanding stakeholder responses are received (see Section 5).

This matrix should be reviewed and re-approved annually, and updated on an event-driven basis following role changes, reorganizations, new entities, material acquisitions, or changes in regulatory requirements

This document is a DRAFT. The following stakeholder inputs are outstanding as of the date of this memorandum. Cells marked [PENDING] in the matrix reflect these gaps. Cells marked [GAP] reflect known control weaknesses with no current authority structure.

Aircraft on Ground. A maintenance or operational condition in which an aircraft is grounded and cannot fly. AOG situations often require time-critical decisions that cannot wait for standard approval chains.
Airworthiness Engineer. An FAA-recognized engineering role within the repair station; part of the Q/A escalation chain above the Chief Inspector and DOM.
Chief Inspector. The senior quality assurance authority within the repair station; responsible for return-to-service approvals under FAR Part 119 and OpSpec A007.
Director of Maintenance. The operational leader of the maintenance department; part of the FAA-regulated escalation chain for airworthiness decisions.

Delegation of Authority. A formal governance document or matrix defining who has authority to approve, commit, or sign off on decisions and up to what dollar amount or scope, by role rather than by individual.

Federal Aviation Regulation Part 119. Governs certification of air carriers and commercial operators, including authority structures for repair stations. The regulatory floor for Q/A and return-to-service authority at Bridger Aerospace.

Fleet Management System (or the entity/division referred to as FMS in the project notes). Identified as operating with contracts and systems entirely outside current oversight; a critical control gap.
Letter of Intent. A document indicating a party’s intention to enter a transaction or agreement; used in BD contexts; subject to TAP routing.
Letter of Understanding. A document type identified by Rob (BD) as in use at Bridger; treated with the same TAP routing discipline as LOIs and MOUs.
Memorandum of Understanding. A formal document outlining the terms of a proposed relationship or transaction; used in BD contexts; subject to TAP routing and CEO approval.
Non-Disclosure Agreement. A confidentiality agreement; must use Legal-approved templates; non-standard terms require Justin Mogford review.
Operations Specification A007. The FAA-issued operating specification listing authorized personnel on the repair station roster; governs who may approve return-to-service.

Sarbanes-Oxley Act. Federal law requiring effective internal controls over financial reporting. Sections 302 and 404 require management certification and are the regulatory basis for dual-approval requirements, segregation of duties, and audit trail requirements in this matrix.
Transaction Approval Process (or Procedure). Bridger’s internal BD transaction approval workflow. Routes through CFO, COO, CEO, Trudy (coordination), and Rob. Full name to be confirmed with Rob.
Approved PO or invoice with GL coding
